posts.text: As someone who spends an unhealthy amount of time on the internet, I get asked for HR resources constantly. Most are a hard pass. This one isn’t. Dan’s HRBP Bootcamp is one of those rare resources that’s actually useful, practical, and created by someone who knows what they’re talking about. So if you’re trying to level up as an HRBP, here’s a recommendation I can make without crossing my fingers behind my back. Link in comments! Daniel Space
posts.hook: As someone who spends an unhealthy amount of time on the internet, I get asked for HR resources constantly.
posts.name: Daniel Space

posts.text: "Ryan Breslow didn't eliminate HR he just called it People Operations" Actually yes he did. The moment he renamed it "People Operations." For two weeks everyone has debated the layoff. Wrong story. Bolt still has people doing HR work. What got eliminated wasn't the department it was the strategic identity of the function. Here's the take I'm holding with both hands: Human Resources is the RIGHT name. We are the resource for the human beings, AND we own the strategies that treat people as what they are to a business — resources. Both things. At once. That's the job. "Human Capital" turns people into cattle. "Personnel" just administers them. And "People Operations"? The moment you put Operations in the title, you've operationalized the function. It stops being a strategic partner and becomes an administrative workflow with a softer brand. Org design. Leadership development. Succession planning. Workforce planning. None of that is "operations" — and none of it survives being treated like operations and none of it is going away with AI. Names aren't semantics. Names are scope. Theres a reason we call it "spell" - because words have power. The full essay is in today's HRBPS issue Genuine question for my HR folks: does your department say HR or People Ops — and when it changed, did anything actually change with it?
posts.hook: "Ryan Breslow didn't eliminate HR he just called it People Operations" Actually yes he did.
posts.name: Daniel Space

posts.text: In the first five real minutes of my day on Friday, in actual daylight consciousness, I lay in bed thinking: I should call Mom. I haven't talked to her in a while. I wonder what she's up to. She has been gone for five years. This is the story of the dream that did that to me... and what I think it meant.
posts.hook: In the first five real minutes of my day on Friday, in actual daylight consciousness, I lay in bed thinking: I should call Mom.
posts.name: Daniel Space

posts.text: Take one step forward if your dad has ever taken you to school. Take one step forward if your dad has ever made you breakfast. Take one step forward if your dad has ever taken you to the park. Take one step forward if your dad has ever told you he loves you. Take one step forward if your dad has ever told you he's proud of you. Take one step forward if your dad has ever had a pillow fight with you. And take one step forward if your dad has ever went on a vacation with you. Now turn around.
posts.hook: Take one step forward if your dad has ever taken you to school.
posts.name: Daniel Space

posts.text: One of the most profoundest changes you can make for your own mental and emotional health is the boundary you create with yourself. Words, especially the words we use with ourselves are everything. As someone with an admittedly high tendency to feel that has had to come with a lot of training and learning, the biggest most powerful lesson is the words we use to describe our emotional state. Though it doesn't seem like a lot, the difference between: "I am (emotion of choice), so happy, excited, jealous, sad, depressed, full of self hatred, in a funk, beyond moved, overjoyed, etc" or "I am FEELING (so happy, excited, jealous, sad, depressed, full of self hatred, in a funk, beyond moved, overjoyed, etc)" is EVERYTHING It creates a very natural and necessary distance between the core being of yourself, that can OBSERVE your emotional state, wonder at it, question it, enjoy it, or react to it, without physically responding or reacting to it. It is an insanely powerful way to remind yourself that feelings are temporary things that you experience, not the guiding powers of your life
posts.hook: One of the most profoundest changes you can make for your own mental and emotional health is the boundary you create with yourself.
posts.name: Daniel Space

posts.text: CHRO Watch: Uber. The company says its HR cuts are not about AI. That may be the most important part. A business growing 25 percent a year just cut nearly a quarter of its People team. Not because revenue fell. Because the function, in its own words, had become too complex. On June 3, Uber confirmed it is reducing its People and Places organization, HR, recruiting, workplace facilities and culture, by 23 percent. That is less than 1 percent of the 34,000-person company, so this is not a distress cut. It is a redesign. CEO Dara Khosrowshahi told staff the changes are necessary to maximize the effectiveness of the People team, and leadership was explicit that this is not about AI. ▸ Read the timing, not just the number. The cut lands three weeks after Jill Hazelbaker took over People and Places inside a broader president role. A new leader inherited the function and her first visible move was to simplify it. That is org design, not cost-cutting. ▸ Notice where People now sits. It reports into a corporate affairs and communications leader, not a standalone CHRO. Whatever the intent, that says something about how the seat is valued, and it raises the premium on Operating Upwards for whoever runs the function day to day. ▸ The AI denial is the tell. Uber says this is not about AI, yet it has reportedly passed its 2026 AI budget in four months and already said it is slowing hiring as it adopts AI internally. You can take the denial at face value and still read the direction of travel. People is being asked to do what it helped finance and engineering do: get leaner and more strategic at once. That makes Business Acumen a baseline expectation for HR, not a differentiator. The CHRO takeaway: when even HR is restructured for efficiency, the question stops being how many people the function has and becomes what the function is for. Every CHRO should have that answer ready before someone above them writes one. Follow for more. #CHROWatch #SuccessionPlanning #PeopleStrategy #Leadership #uber
posts.name: Daniel Space

posts.text: The metaphor itself is deeply primal. Shaping wet clay is the ultimate act of raw, physical creation: taking formless, chaotic earth and forcing it into order with your bare hands. Overlaying that heavy, physical labor with a story about a literal ghost (pure, disembodied spirit) is why the scene anchored itself so deeply into our collective psyche. It perfectly captured the intersection of the tangible material world and the intangible human soul. We mime the wheel because our subconscious recognizes the ancient, universal language of creation and connection.
posts.hook: The metaphor itself is deeply primal.
posts.name: Daniel Space

posts.text: In 2021, my mother was diagnosed with terminal cancer, and I became one of her primary caretakers until she passed. After her death, my world went entirely dark. I spent months physically locked inside a basement apartment, and that phone screen and TikTok app became my only remaining bridge to the living world. I forged real, deep friendships there - people I still value immensely, who made a brutally isolated chapter feel slightly less lonely. (Thank you @ Rob Cancilla , Amy Miller Farah Sharghi Tessa White , Francis Rhodes, aPHR , Jamie Jackson Ashley Herd Tianna Treichelt Veronica Gidstedt Jackie Cuevas Jack Appleby Bernadette Pawlik Tejal Rives -and a whole bunch of other wonderful people that I can't remember right now)
posts.hook: In 2021, my mother was diagnosed with terminal cancer, and I became one of her primary caretakers until she passed.
posts.name: Daniel Space

posts.text: That experience permanently altered my perspective on how ineffective most corporate harassment remediation truly is. The system treated the situation as a liability-management exercise instead of a human one. The training did not create empathy. It did not create accountability. It merely taught someone how to survive the process and left 8 people who suffered the actions to their own devices of a "sorry about that, next time don't go to business events at bards" feeling.
posts.hook: That experience permanently altered my perspective on how ineffective most corporate harassment remediation truly is.
posts.name: Daniel Space

posts.text: Most HR Business Partners spend the first part of their careers being rewarded for responsiveness. Someone escalates a conflict, a leader needs a hire approved, a reorg is messy, a team is spiraling, and the HRBP arrives with solutions in hand like a corporate paramedic carrying a clipboard instead of a crash kit - and in our eager desire to be liked and appreciated, we take it all on.
posts.hook: Most HR Business Partners spend the first part of their careers being rewarded for responsiveness.
posts.name: Daniel Space

posts.text: You do not need to become technical, nor do you need to view people solely as data points, but you should understand: the difference between analytics, insights, research and data science, what and how the data organization is supporting and how it's evolving in addition to fun words like statistical anomaly and outlier
posts.hook: You do not need to become technical, nor do you need to view people solely as data points, but you should understand: the difference between…
posts.name: Daniel Space

posts.text: I give a concise, high-level overview of my experience by focusing on key outcomes across roles. I’ll highlight things like building a 200-person data and analytics organization, rolling out internal mobility at Spotify, or designing a compensation model at WebMD so the interviewer immediately understands my scope and impact.
posts.hook: I give a concise, high-level overview of my experience by focusing on key outcomes across roles.
posts.name: Daniel Space

posts.text: I did not have a roadmap when I stepped into this role. I learned through experience, mistakes, and working alongside strong leaders. Over time, patterns became clearer, and the role itself became more defined. Most HRBPs do not get that level of exposure or support, which is why so many are left trying to figure it out on their own.
posts.hook: I did not have a roadmap when I stepped into this role.
posts.name: Daniel Space

posts.text: That’s the point where something shifted for me. My sister in christ, what information should we share? That we had been pushing back and escalating the entire time and were fed lies to tell the business? That it was obviously a poorly thought out exercise? That Epic was so stupidly self-sabotaging in its control of information? That giving feedback and providing real performance notes was not something the company could do? The worst part was that another executive had to come to her rescue. She never once owned the very good points employees were making, but she did thank the executive for coming to her defense. Working wth data scientists I could easily predict the bonus that she got, small price to pay for your integrity.
posts.hook: That’s the point where something shifted for me.
posts.name: Daniel Space
